title: Small Ladder Bookshelf
summary: Three open plywood-bottomed trays leaning against an angled 1x2 ladder frame — a small leaning bookshelf sized for a nightstand or kids' room.
description:
The three trays are graduated in size from top to bottom, and each is really just a shallow open box with a plywood bottom — nothing complicated there. The ladder frame is what carries the visual interest and the mild engineering challenge: the two long rails are cut at a 10° angle so the whole thing leans against a wall by design rather than standing bolt upright, and the shorter mounting pieces get a matching 10° angle on one end to seat flush against those leaning rails.

Build the leg/rail assembly in mirror image on both sides — drilling the pocket holes on opposite faces so they end up hidden on the inside once the ladder frame is assembled, the same technique used on any piece where two mirrored sides need to hide their joinery from each other.

tools.note: Only needed for any decorative cutouts.

cutList.note: Cut to match each tray: 9", 11", and 13-1/2" respectively.
Both ends cut at 10° off square, ends parallel — long point to short point measurement.
One end cut at 10° off square, longest point measurement.
One end cut at 10° off square, longest point measurement.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: From the 1x4 cut four tray sides at 22-1/2", two small tray ends at 9" and two medium tray ends at 11". From the 1x6 cut two large-tray sides at 22-1/2" and two large-tray ends at 13-1/2". From the 1x2 cut two straight ladder rails at 30", two angled ladder rails at 30-1/2" with BOTH ends cut at 10° off square and parallel to each other (long point to short point measurement), two small cross pieces at 9-1/2" and two large cross pieces at 5-1/2", each cross piece with one end cut at 10° measured to its longest point so it seats flush against an angled rail. Cut the three plywood bottoms 24" long and 9", 11" and 13-1/2" wide to match their trays. Set the miter saw once for the 10° and cut all six angled pieces at that setting rather than resetting between them — every one of those angles has to agree or the ladder will not lean flat against a wall.

steps.title: Build the three trays
steps.body: Each tray is a shallow open box built the same way: the two end pieces run the full depth and the two 22-1/2" sides fit between them, which puts the finished tray at exactly 24" long — the length of the plywood bottoms. Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of each side piece, on the face that will end up underneath, and pull the joints together with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Build the small tray with the two 9" ends, the medium with the 11" ends and the deep bottom tray with the 13-1/2" 1x6 ends and the 1x6 sides. Check each tray's diagonals while the glue is wet, then glue a plywood bottom onto the underside of each and fix it with 3/4" brads or staples — the bottom is what locks the tray square for good.

steps.title: Build the two ladder sides in mirror
steps.body: Each side of the ladder is a flat frame: one 30-1/2" rail with 10° cuts at both ends, which is the leaning member that meets the wall and the floor, one straight 30" rail, and the 9-1/2" and 5-1/2" cross pieces joining them, each cross piece presenting its 10° end to the angled rail so the two seat flush. Lay the first side out on the bench, mark where each cross piece lands, drill 3/4" pocket holes in the cross pieces and pull it together with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Then build the second side as a MIRROR of the first — lay it out facing the first rather than copying it, and drill its pocket holes on the opposite face, so that once the two frames face each other every hole ends up on the inside and none shows from outside. The plan gives only two cross pieces per side for three trays and does not say what height each tray sits at, so set the cross pieces where the tray depths suit you and record the positions before building the second side.

steps.title: Hang the trays and finish
steps.body: Stand the two ladder sides on edge facing each other and use a square to mark matching tray heights on the inside face of each, deepest tray at the bottom and shallowest at the top so the whole thing tapers with the lean. Because the trays are all 24" long, they set the width of the shelf themselves: hold each tray between the sides and drive 1-1/4" screws through the rails into the tray ends, two per joint, starting with the bottom tray and working up so the frame stays parallel. Check that all three trays read level to each other while the ladder is leaned at its working angle, not while it is lying flat — a leaning frame that was squared flat can still sit crooked against the wall. Sand every face and edge, ease the corners, and finish; then lean it against a wall so the angled rails bear flat and load the bottom tray first.

images.alt: Small leaning ladder bookshelf with three graduated trays
